title: The Advantages and Disadvantages of Working from Home
prompt: Remote working has become far more common in recent years. Write an essay discussing the advantages and disadvantages of working from home. Consider its impact on productivity, work-life balance, communication, and mental health. End with your own view on whether remote working is, overall, a positive development.
format: Advantages and disadvantages essay · neutral/formal · 150–250 words

examples.title: Working from Home: Good or Bad?
examples.body:
In recent years, many people have started working from home because of new technology and changes in the workplace. There are both advantages and disadvantages to this way of working.

One advantage is that people save time because they do not need to travel to the office. This means they can spend more time with their family or do other activities. Working from home can also help people feel more relaxed and comfortable.

However, there are also some disadvantages. It can be difficult to concentrate at home because of noise or other distractions. Some people also feel lonely because they do not see their colleagues every day. Communication can be harder when everyone is in a different place.

In my opinion, working from home can be a good choice if you have a quiet space and good organisation. However, I think it is important to go to the office sometimes in order to stay connected with your team.

Overall, remote working has real benefits, but it works best when there is a healthy balance between home and office time.

examples.title: Remote Working: Freedom or Isolation?
examples.body:
The rapid expansion of remote working has fundamentally changed the way millions of people approach their professional lives. While this shift offers genuine benefits, it also presents challenges that deserve careful consideration.

On the positive side, working from home eliminates the daily commute, returning several hours a week that employees can redirect towards family, exercise, or personal development. Many workers also report higher productivity at home, where they can structure their day around their natural working rhythm without the constant interruptions typical of open-plan offices.

Nevertheless, the disadvantages are equally significant. The blurring of boundaries between work and personal life means some employees struggle to switch off, leading to longer hours and increased stress over time. The absence of spontaneous face-to-face interaction can also erode team cohesion and contribute to feelings of isolation and disengagement.

Furthermore, not everyone has access to a suitable home working environment. Those in shared or noisy accommodation may find concentration difficult, creating an inequality of experience that employers do not always acknowledge.

In conclusion, remote working is neither inherently good nor bad – its impact depends largely on the individual and the organisation. A hybrid model combining the flexibility of home working with the collaborative energy of the office appears to offer the most sustainable solution for the majority of knowledge workers.
